findings.title: No centralized authentication service or identity provider defined
findings.description: The task plan describes RBAC and JWT-based auth as decision points (dp-7) but there is no dedicated task for implementing a centralized authentication service, identity provider, or OAuth2/OIDC server. Individual services (Equipment Catalog task 2006, RMS, Finance) implement their own JWT verification, but there is no single source of truth for user identity, token issuance, or session management. This leads to fragmented auth logic, inconsistent token validation, and difficulty in revoking access across services.
findings.recommendation: Add a dedicated task to deploy an identity provider (e.g., Keycloak, Zitadel, or Auth0) or implement a shared auth service that handles user registration, login, token issuance (JWT/OIDC), and token revocation. All services should validate tokens against this central provider rather than implementing independent JWT verification.

findings.title: Stripe webhook secret and payment data handling lacks explicit security controls
findings.description: Task 4003 implements Stripe webhook handling and payment processing but does not explicitly address PCI DSS considerations. The plan stores payment_intent_id and processes financial transactions. While Stripe handles most PCI scope, the webhook endpoint must verify signatures, and the service must ensure no raw card data is logged or stored. There is no mention of ensuring the webhook endpoint is only accessible from Stripe IP ranges or through signature verification at the network level.
findings.recommendation: 1) Ensure Stripe webhook signature verification is implemented (partially noted in 4003 but should be mandatory, not just mentioned). 2) Add a NetworkPolicy or WAF rule to restrict the webhook endpoint to Stripe's published IP ranges. 3) Add explicit PII/payment data logging exclusion rules in the audit logging configuration. 4) Document PCI DSS scope and controls.

findings.title: PII handling and encryption at rest not addressed for customer and vetting data
findings.description: The Customer Vetting service (Task 5) stores sensitive business and personal data from OpenCorporates, LinkedIn, Google Reviews, and credit reports. The Finance service stores invoice and payroll data. The RMS stores customer information. None of these tasks specify encryption at rest for the PostgreSQL database, field-level encryption for sensitive PII fields, or data retention/deletion policies.
findings.recommendation: 1) Enable PostgreSQL encryption at rest via the CloudNative-PG operator's storage encryption or underlying volume encryption. 2) Implement field-level encryption for highly sensitive fields (credit scores, personal identifiers, payroll data) using application-level encryption before database storage. 3) Define and implement data retention policies with automated cleanup jobs. 4) Add a task for GDPR/data protection compliance review.

findings.title: File upload sanitization not specified for social media photo uploads
findings.description: Task 6002 implements photo upload accepting multipart/form-data with file type validation (JPEG, PNG, WebP) and size limits, but does not mention image content validation, malware scanning, EXIF metadata stripping (which can contain GPS coordinates and PII), or protection against image-based exploits (polyglot files, zip bombs disguised as images).
findings.recommendation: 1) Strip EXIF metadata from uploaded images before storage (use a library like sharp or exiftool). 2) Re-encode uploaded images to sanitize them (convert to a clean format). 3) Validate image headers match declared content type (not just file extension). 4) Consider adding ClamAV or similar malware scanning for uploads. 5) Implement rate limiting on the upload endpoint specifically.

findings.title: Morgan AI agent has broad unrestricted access to all backend services
findings.description: Task 7005 registers MCP tools giving Morgan access to catalog search, customer vetting, quote creation, invoice submission, finance reports, social media posting, RMS operations, and admin user management. There is no mention of scoped permissions per conversation context, per-user authorization delegation, or rate limiting on tool invocations. A compromised Morgan agent (or prompt injection attack) could invoke any backend operation.
findings.recommendation: 1) Implement per-tool authorization that validates the end-user's permissions before executing a tool call (Morgan should delegate the caller's identity, not use a super-admin service account). 2) Add rate limiting on MCP tool invocations per session/user. 3) Implement an allow-list of tools per skill context (e.g., the sales-qual skill should not have access to admin tools). 4) Add prompt injection detection/mitigation in the agent's input processing pipeline. 5) Log all tool invocations with the originating user context for audit.

findings.title: No explicit protection against SQL injection in raw SQL migrations and queries
findings.description: Multiple services use raw SQL migrations (tasks 2001, 3001, 4001, 5001, 6001) and database queries. While SQLx (Rust) and pgx (Go) provide parameterized queries, the plan does not explicitly mandate parameterized queries or mention SQL injection prevention. The grpc-gateway REST layer in the RMS service (Task 3) accepts user input that flows into database queries.
findings.recommendation: 1) Add explicit guidance in implementation tasks to always use parameterized queries (SQLx compile-time checked queries in Rust, pgx parameterized queries in Go). 2) Enable SQL query logging in development to detect any string concatenation in queries. 3) Add integration tests that specifically test SQL injection payloads against user-facing endpoints.

findings.title: Secrets stored as plain Kubernetes Secrets without external secret store integration
findings.description: Task 1007 creates Kubernetes Secrets using `kubectl create secret generic` which stores secrets as base64-encoded values (not encrypted) in etcd. The decision point dp-12 for external secrets management is unresolved and marked as 'open'. Task 10002-10003 address rotation but depend on an external secret store being chosen. Until this is resolved, all API keys, database credentials, and service tokens are stored in plainly encoded Kubernetes Secrets.
findings.recommendation: 1) Resolve dp-12 early in the project — strongly recommend adopting external-secrets.io with a backing store (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or similar). 2) Enable etcd encryption at rest in the Kubernetes cluster as an immediate measure. 3) Ensure RBAC restricts Secret read access (covered in 10001 but should be prioritized alongside secret creation).

findings.title: Container image provenance and scanning not addressed
findings.description: Task 2007 creates a Dockerfile for the Equipment Catalog service and other tasks implicitly create container images, but there is no task for container image scanning, signing, or ensuring images come from trusted base registries. The Signal-CLI deployment (1005) uses `bbernhard/signal-cli-rest-api` which is a community image without verified provenance.
findings.recommendation: 1) Add a task for integrating container image scanning into the CI/CD pipeline (Trivy, Grype, or Snyk Container). 2) Pin all base images to specific digests (not just tags). 3) For third-party images like Signal-CLI, verify the image source, scan it, and consider rebuilding from source. 4) Implement a Kubernetes admission controller (e.g., Kyverno or OPA Gatekeeper) to enforce image policies (signed images only, approved registries only).

findings.title: Dependency versions not pinned and no dependency auditing task
findings.description: The task plan specifies minimum versions (Rust 1.75+, Axum 0.7, Go 1.22+, Next.js 15) but does not mention pinning exact dependency versions in lock files, running dependency audit tools (cargo audit, npm audit, govulncheck), or establishing a process for security patch management.
findings.recommendation: 1) Add explicit steps in each project initialization task to run the appropriate dependency audit tool (cargo audit for Rust, govulncheck for Go, npm audit for Node.js) and address any findings. 2) Mandate the use of lock files (Cargo.lock, go.sum, bun.lockb) committed to the repo. 3) Add a recurring CI job or task for dependency vulnerability scanning. 4) Pin transitive dependencies where possible.

findings.title: Stripe webhook endpoint exposed without network-level protection
findings.description: The Finance service webhook endpoint (POST /api/v1/webhooks/stripe) will be publicly accessible. While task 4003 mentions signature verification, a sophisticated attacker could still attempt DoS attacks against this endpoint or exploit timing side-channels in signature verification.
findings.recommendation: 1) Implement constant-time signature comparison for Stripe webhook verification. 2) Add rate limiting specifically on the webhook endpoint. 3) Consider restricting the endpoint at the network/ingress level to Stripe's published IP ranges (https://stripe.com/docs/ips). 4) Add monitoring/alerting for unusual webhook volume.

findings.title: No security incident response plan or runbook defined
findings.description: While task 10006 creates security monitoring dashboards and alerting rules, and task 10007 creates documentation, there is no explicit task for creating a security incident response plan covering: breach notification procedures, credential compromise response, service isolation procedures, or forensic evidence preservation.
findings.recommendation: Add to task 10007 or create a separate subtask for developing a security incident response plan covering: 1) Incident classification (severity levels), 2) Response procedures per incident type (credential compromise, data breach, DDoS, unauthorized access), 3) Communication templates and escalation paths, 4) Evidence preservation procedures (log retention, snapshot creation), 5) Post-incident review process.

findings.title: Signal-CLI phone number registration credentials lack adequate protection
findings.description: Task 1005 stores Signal registration credentials in a Kubernetes Secret, but Signal-CLI registration state is also stored on a PersistentVolumeClaim. If the PVC is compromised or the pod is accessed, an attacker could impersonate the Morgan agent's Signal identity, sending messages to all contacts.
findings.recommendation: 1) Encrypt the Signal-CLI data directory on the PVC (use an encrypted StorageClass or application-level encryption). 2) Restrict access to the Signal-CLI pod with tight NetworkPolicies (only Morgan agent can reach it). 3) Implement monitoring for unexpected Signal message patterns. 4) Have a documented procedure for revoking and re-registering the Signal number if compromised.

findings.title: ConfigMaps containing connection strings are replicated across all namespaces
findings.description: Task 1006 creates the sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap in every service namespace. While ConfigMaps don't contain credentials directly (those are in Secrets), the connection strings may reveal internal service topology (hostnames, ports, database names) which could aid an attacker who gains access to any namespace.
findings.recommendation: 1) Ensure ConfigMaps only contain the connection information needed by services in that specific namespace (least-privilege principle). 2) Consider using per-namespace ConfigMaps with only the endpoints that namespace's services need, rather than a blanket copy. 3) Combine with NetworkPolicies (task 9006) to ensure services can only reach the endpoints they need.

findings.title: Web frontend API calls lack CSRF protection details
findings.description: Task 8005 (quote builder) and 8006 (chat widget) make API calls to backend services. While Next.js provides some built-in protections, the plan doesn't address CSRF tokens for state-changing operations (quote submission) or origin validation for WebSocket connections.
findings.recommendation: 1) Implement CSRF tokens for the quote submission form using Next.js server actions or a custom token mechanism. 2) Validate the Origin header on WebSocket upgrade requests for the Morgan chat endpoint. 3) Configure appropriate SameSite cookie attributes if session cookies are used.

findings.title: Database connections not explicitly configured for TLS
findings.description: The infrastructure tasks (1002, 1003) set up PostgreSQL and Redis but do not mention enabling TLS for database connections. The ConfigMap connection strings (1006) don't specify sslmode=require for PostgreSQL or TLS for Redis.
findings.recommendation: 1) Enable TLS on the PostgreSQL cluster via CloudNative-PG's TLS configuration (it supports automatic certificate management). 2) Configure Redis TLS via the operator's TLS settings. 3) Ensure all connection strings in ConfigMaps include sslmode=verify-full for PostgreSQL and TLS parameters for Redis. 4) Use the operator-managed CA certificates for verification.

findings.title: Pod security standards not enforced
findings.description: No task mentions Kubernetes Pod Security Standards (PSS) or Pod Security Admission. Without these, pods could run as root, mount the host filesystem, or use privileged containers, expanding the blast radius of any container escape.
findings.recommendation: 1) Add labels to all namespaces to enforce Pod Security Standards at the 'restricted' level: `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted`. 2) Ensure all Deployment manifests specify securityContext with runAsNonRoot: true, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true (where feasible), drop all capabilities, and set allowPrivilegeEscalation: false. 3) Test that the Signal-CLI container works under restricted PSS (may need adjustment).

findings.title: Google Calendar API credentials grant overly broad access risk
findings.description: Task 3004 and 3006 integrate with Google Calendar API. Decision point dp-8 discusses service account vs. OAuth2 flow. If a service account with domain-wide delegation is used (one of the options), a compromised RMS service could access any calendar in the organization's Google Workspace domain.
findings.recommendation: 1) Prefer OAuth2 user consent flow over domain-wide delegation to limit blast radius. 2) If service account is chosen, scope it to the minimum required calendar(s) only. 3) Store Google credentials in a dedicated, tightly-scoped Kubernetes Secret accessible only to the RMS service. 4) Audit calendar API access logs regularly.

findings.title: No log tamper protection or immutability guarantee
findings.description: Tasks 10004 and 10005 ship audit logs to Loki, but there is no mention of log integrity protection. An attacker with access to the logging pipeline could potentially modify or delete audit evidence.
findings.recommendation: 1) Configure Loki with append-only storage (e.g., S3 with Object Lock or immutable bucket policies). 2) Consider cryptographic log chaining or signing for critical audit events. 3) Set retention policies that prevent deletion within the compliance-required period.

strengths: Comprehensive audit logging is planned at both Kubernetes API level (10004) and application level (10005, 3008), covering the full stack
Network policies (9006, 15) with default-deny approach demonstrate defense-in-depth thinking
Secret rotation is explicitly planned (10002, 10003) with both automated and manual procedures considered
Kubernetes RBAC is thoroughly addressed (10001) with least-privilege principles and per-service ServiceAccounts
Health and readiness probes are consistently implemented across all services (2007, 3009, 4007, 5008, 6008), enabling proper orchestration and failure detection
Stripe webhook signature verification and idempotency are explicitly planned (4003)
End-to-end testing is planned for every service (2007, 3010, 4008, 5009, 6009, 7010), which helps catch security issues early
The plan includes HA for all stateful components (PostgreSQL, Redis) with automatic failover testing
summary: The task plan has a solid foundation for infrastructure security with comprehensive audit logging, network policies, RBAC, and HA configurations. However, it has high-severity gaps in centralized authentication (no identity provider), PII/data-at-rest encryption, and the Morgan AI agent's unrestricted access to all backend services. The most critical immediate actions are: implementing a centralized identity provider, enforcing encryption at rest for sensitive data, adding authorization scoping for the AI agent's tool invocations, and resolving the secret management decision point early to avoid plaintext secret storage in etcd.
